screening_results.reason: This is a task-based fMRI study that conducted group-level whole-brain analyses (whole-brain contrasts and multiple regressions) of a propositional analogy task, which qualifies as multi-step relational/problem-solving. It includes a separate healthy adult group (n=17, ages 19–26) reported separately from children. The paper reports peak coordinates for key contrasts/regions (e.g., left RLPFC −27,50,0; right RLPFC 27,45,16; SPL 36,−66,52). Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the review.

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult participants (ages 20–40) that uses novel multi-step nonverbal reasoning problems (rule derivation and application), satisfying problem-solving task requirements. Group-level whole-brain random-effects analyses were conducted (SPM5) with FDR correction and whole-brain contrasts reported. Peak activation coordinates for multiple contrasts (rule complexity, analogical distance, task > baseline) are provided across frontal, parietal, and occipital regions. Participants are healthy adults (two experiments, n=16 and n=21), and task effects within the healthy groups are reported. The paper is empirical and not a review. Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria and violates no exclusion criteria.

This study reports a task-based fMRI experiment using the Raven's Progressive Matrices (a multi-step visuospatial/logical problem-solving task) with group-level whole-brain analyses and reports coordinates for task contrasts and seed regions. It includes a healthy comparison (non-autistic) group reported separately and within-group task effects are presented. Therefore it satisfies the review inclusion requirements for task-based whole-brain fMRI of problem-solving and coordinate reporting. Note: participant ages span 14–36; although some participants are adolescents, a healthy adult comparison group is reported separately (mean age ~21.7). If the review applies a strict minimum age cutoff (all participants >=18), this could reduce confidence; otherwise the study is eligible.

This study meets all inclusion criteria for the review: it is a task-based fMRI experiment with group-level whole-brain analyses (voxel-based contrasts and cluster-level inference), employs a multi-step problem-solving task (Raven’s Standard Progressive Matrices; analytic items requiring analogical reasoning), includes a healthy adult control group (NC; ages matched to ASC) reported separately, and reports peak locations in MNI space for contrasts. It does not violate exclusion criteria (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state only, not a review, and presents within-group task effects for healthy controls). Therefore it is eligible for inclusion.
